title: The Eve Of St. Agnes
content:
I

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ St. Agnesן¿½ Eve Ah, bitter chill it was!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The owl, for all his feathers, was a-cold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hare limpן¿½d trembling through the frozen grass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And silent was the flock in woolly fold:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Numb were the Beadsmanן¿½s fingers, while he told
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His rosary, and while his frosted breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like pious incense from a censer old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seemן¿½d taking flight for heaven, without a death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Past the sweet Virginן¿½s picture, while his prayer he saith.

II

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His prayer he saith, this patient, holy man;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then takes his lamp, and riseth from his knees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And back returneth, meagre, barefoot, wan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Along the chapel aisle by slow degrees:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sculpturן¿½d dead, on each side, seem to freeze,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Emprisonן¿½d in black, purgatorial rails:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Knights, ladies, praying in dumb oratן¿½ries,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He passeth by; and his weak spirit fails
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To think how they may ache in icy hoods and mails.

III

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Northward he turneth through a little door,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And scarce three steps, ere Musicן¿½s golden tongue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flatterן¿½d to tears this aged man and poor;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But no already had his deathbell rung;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The joys of all his life were said and sung:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His was harsh penance on St. Agnesן¿½ Eve:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Another way he went, and soon among
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rough ashes sat he for his soulן¿½s reprieve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all night kept awake, for sinnersן¿½ sake to grieve.

IV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That ancient Beadsman heard the prelude soft;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so it chancן¿½d, for many a door was wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From hurry to and fro. Soon, up aloft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The silver, snarling trumpets ן¿½gan to chide:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The level chambers, ready with their pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were glowing to receive a thousand guests:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The carved angels, ever eager-eyed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Starן¿½d, where upon their heads the cornice rests,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With hair blown back, and wings put cross-wise on their breasts.

V

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At length burst in the argent revelry,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With plume, tiara, and all rich array,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Numerous as shadows haunting fairily
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brain, new stuff d, in youth, with triumphs gay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of old romance. These let us wish away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And turn, sole-thoughted, to one Lady there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose heart had brooded, all that wintry day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On love, and wingן¿½d St. Agnesן¿½ saintly care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As she had heard old dames full many times declare.

VI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They told her how, upon St. Agnesן¿½ Eve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young virgins might have visions of delight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And soft adorings from their loves receive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon the honeyן¿½d middle of the night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If ceremonies due they did aright;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As, supperless to bed they must retire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And couch supine their beauties, lily white;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor look behind, nor sideways, but require
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Heaven with upward eyes for all that they desire.

VII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Full of this whim was thoughtful Madeline:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The music, yearning like a God in pain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She scarcely heard: her maiden eyes divine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fixן¿½d on the floor, saw many a sweeping train
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pass by she heeded not at all: in vain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came many a tiptoe, amorous cavalier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And back retirן¿½d; not coolן¿½d by high disdain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But she saw not: her heart was otherwhere:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She sighן¿½d for Agnesן¿½ dreams, the sweetest of the year.

VIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She dancן¿½d along with vague, regardless eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anxious her lips, her breathing quick and short:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hallowן¿½d hour was near at hand: she sighs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Amid the timbrels, and the throngן¿½d resort
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of whisperers in anger, or in sport;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mid looks of love, defiance, hate, and scorn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hoodwinkן¿½d with faery fancy; all amort,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save to St. Agnes and her lambs unshorn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the bliss to be before to-morrow morn.

IX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, purposing each moment to retire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She lingerן¿½d still. Meantime, across the moors,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had come young Porphyro, with heart on fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Madeline. Beside the portal doors,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Buttressן¿½d from moonlight, stands he, and implores
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All saints to give him sight of Madeline,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But for one moment in the tedious hours,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That he might gaze and worship all unseen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perchance speak, kneel, touch, kiss in sooth such things have been.

X

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He ventures in: let no buzzן¿½d whisper tell:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All eyes be muffled, or a hundred swords
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will storm his heart, Loveן¿½s fevן¿½rous citadel:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For him, those chambers held barbarian hordes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hyena foemen, and hot-blooded lords,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose very dogs would execrations howl
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against his lineage: not one breast affords
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Him any mercy, in that mansion foul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save one old beldame, weak in body and in soul.

XI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, happy chance! the aged creature came,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shuffling along with ivory-headed wand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To where he stood, hid from the torchן¿½s flame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Behind a broad hall-pillar, far beyond
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sound of merriment and chorus bland:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He startled her; but soon she knew his face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And graspן¿½d his fingers in her palsied hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saying, ן¿½Mercy, Porphyro! hie thee from this place;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They are all here to-night, the whole blood-thirsty race!

XII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Get hence! get hence! thereן¿½s dwarfish Hildebrand;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He had a fever late, and in the fit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He cursed thee and thine, both house and land:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then thereן¿½s that old Lord Maurice, not a whit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½More tame for his gray hairs, Alas me! flit!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Flit like a ghost away.ן¿½ ן¿½Ah, Gossip dear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weן¿½re safe enough; here in this arm-chair sit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And tell me howן¿½ ן¿½Good Saints! not here, not here;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Follow me, child, or else these stones will be thy bier.ן¿½

XIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He followן¿½d through a lowly arched way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brushing the cobwebs with his lofty plume;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as she mutterן¿½d ן¿½Well-a-well-a-day!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He found him in a little moonlight room,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pale, latticן¿½d, chill, and silent as a tomb.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now tell me where is Madeline,ן¿½ said he,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O tell me, Angela, by the holy loom
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which none but secret sisterhood may see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When they St. Agnesן¿½ wool are weaving piously.ן¿½

XIV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½St. Agnes! Ah! it is St. Agnesן¿½ Eve
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet men will murder upon holy days:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou must hold water in a witchן¿½s sieve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And be liege-lord of all the Elves and Fays,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To venture so: it fills me with amaze
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To see thee, Porphyro! St. Agnesן¿½ Eve!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Godן¿½s help! my lady fair the conjuror plays
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This very night: good angels her deceive!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But let me laugh awhile, Iן¿½ve mickle time to grieve.ן¿½

XV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feebly she laugheth in the languid moon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While Porphyro upon her face doth look,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like puzzled urchin on an aged crone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who keepeth closן¿½d a wondן¿½rous riddle-book,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As spectacled she sits in chimney nook.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But soon his eyes grew brilliant, when she told
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His ladyן¿½s purpose; and he scarce could brook
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tears, at the thought of those enchantments cold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Madeline asleep in lap of legends old.

XVI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sudden a thought came like a full-blown rose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flushing his brow, and in his pained heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made purple riot: then doth he propose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A stratagem, that makes the beldame start:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A cruel man and impious thou art:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sweet lady, let her pray, and sleep, and dream
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Alone with her good angels, far apart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From wicked men like thee. Go, go! I deem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou canst not surely be the same that thou didst seem.

XVII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I will not harm her, by all saints I swear,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quoth Porphyro: ן¿½O may I neן¿½er find grace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When my weak voice shall whisper its last prayer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If one of her soft ringlets I displace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or look with ruffian passion in her face:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Good Angela, believe me by these tears;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or I will, even in a momentן¿½s space,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Awake, with horrid shout, my foemenן¿½s ears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And beard them, though they be more fangן¿½d than wolves and bears.ן¿½

XVIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ah! why wilt thou affright a feeble soul?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A poor, weak, palsy-stricken, churchyard thing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whose passing-bell may ere the midnight toll;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whose prayers for thee, each morn and evening,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were never missן¿½d.ן¿½ Thus plaining, doth she bring
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A gentler speech from burning Porphyro;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So woful, and of such deep sorrowing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Angela gives promise she will do
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatever he shall wish, betide her weal or woe.

XIX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which was, to lead him, in close secrecy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even to Madelineן¿½s chamber, and there hide
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Him in a closet, of such privacy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That he might see her beauty unespied,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And win perhaps that night a peerless bride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While legionן¿½d fairies pacן¿½d the coverlet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pale enchantment held her sleepy-eyed.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never on such a night have lovers met,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since Merlin paid his Demon all the monstrous debt.

XX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½It shall be as thou wishest,ן¿½ said the Dame:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All cates and dainties shall be stored there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Quickly on this feast-night: by the tambour frame
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Her own lute thou wilt see: no time to spare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For I am slow and feeble, and scarce dare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On such a catering trust my dizzy head.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Wait here, my child, with patience; kneel in prayer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The while: Ah! thou must needs the lady wed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or may I never leave my grave among the dead.ן¿½

XXI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So saying, she hobbled off with busy fear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The loverן¿½s endless minutes slowly passן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dame returnן¿½d, and whisperן¿½d in his ear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To follow her; with aged eyes aghast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From fright of dim espial. Safe at last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through many a dusky gallery, they gain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The maidenן¿½s chamber, silken, hushן¿½d, and chaste;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where Porphyro took covert, pleasן¿½d amain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His poor guide hurried back with agues in her brain.

XXII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her faltן¿½ring hand upon the balustrade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Angela was feeling for the stair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Madeline, St. Agnesן¿½ charmed maid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rose, like a missionן¿½d spirit, unaware:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With silver taperן¿½s light, and pious care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She turnן¿½d, and down the aged gossip led
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To a safe level matting. Now prepare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Young Porphyro, for gazing on that bed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She comes, she comes again, like ring-dove frayן¿½d and fled.

XXIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out went the taper as she hurried in;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its little smoke, in pallid moonshine, died:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She closן¿½d the door, she panted, all akin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To spirits of the air, and visions wide:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No uttered syllable, or, woe betide!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But to her heart, her heart was voluble,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paining with eloquence her balmy side;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As though a tongueless nightingale should swell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her throat in vain, and die, heart-stifled, in her dell.

XXIV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A casement high and triple-archן¿½d there was,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All garlanded with carven imagן¿½ries
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of fruits, and flowers, and bunches of knot-grass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And diamonded with panes of quaint device,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Innumerable of stains and splendid dyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As are the tiger-mothן¿½s deep-damaskן¿½d wings;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in the midst, ן¿½mong thousand heraldries,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And twilight saints, and dim emblazonings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A shielded scutcheon blushן¿½d with blood of queens and kings.

XXV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Full on this casement shone the wintry moon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And threw warm gules on Madelineן¿½s fair breast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As down she knelt for heavenן¿½s grace and boon;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rose-bloom fell on her hands, together prest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on her silver cross soft amethyst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on her hair a glory, like a saint:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She seemן¿½d a splendid angel, newly drest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save wings, for heaven: Porphyro grew faint:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She knelt, so pure a thing, so free from mortal taint.

XXVI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Anon his heart revives: her vespers done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of all its wreathed pearls her hair she frees;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unclasps her warmed jewels one by one;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loosens her fragrant boddice; by degrees
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her rich attire creeps rustling to her knees:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Half-hidden, like a mermaid in sea-weed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pensive awhile she dreams awake, and sees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In fancy, fair St. Agnes in her bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But dares not look behind, or all the charm is fled.

XXVII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soon, trembling in her soft and chilly nest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In sort of wakeful swoon, perplexן¿½d she lay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until the poppied warmth of sleep oppressן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her soothed limbs, and soul fatigued away;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flown, like a thought, until the morrow-day;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blissfully havenן¿½d both from joy and pain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Claspן¿½d like a missal where swart Paynims pray;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blinded alike from sunshine and from rain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As though a rose should shut, and be a bud again.

XXVIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stolן¿½n to this paradise, and so entranced,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Porphyro gazed upon her empty dress,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And listenן¿½d to her breathing, if it chanced
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wake into a slumberous tenderness;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which when he heard, that minute did he bless,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And breathן¿½d himself: then from the closet crept,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Noiseless as fear in a wide wilderness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And over the hushן¿½d carpet, silent, stept,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½tween the curtains peepן¿½d, where, lo! how fast she slept.

XXIX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then by the bed-side, where the faded moon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made a dim, silver twilight, soft he set
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A table, and, half anguishן¿½d, threw thereon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A cloth of woven crimson, gold, and jet:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ O for some drowsy Morphean amulet!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The boisterous, midnight, festive clarion,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The kettle-drum, and far-heard clarionet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Affray his ears, though but in dying tone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hall door shuts again, and all the noise is gone.

XXX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And still she slept an azure-lidded sleep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In blanched linen, smooth, and lavenderן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While he from forth the closet brought a heap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of candied apple, quince, and plum, and gourd;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With jellies soother than the creamy curd,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lucent syrops, tinct with cinnamon;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Manna and dates, in argosy transferrן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Fez; and spiced dainties, every one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From silken Samarcand to cedarן¿½d Lebanon.

XXXI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These delicates he heapן¿½d with glowing hand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On golden dishes and in baskets bright
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of wreathed silver: sumptuous they stand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the retired quiet of the night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Filling the chilly room with perfume light.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And now, my love, my seraph fair, awake!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thou art my heaven, and I thine eremite:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Open thine eyes, for meek St. Agnesן¿½ sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or I shall drowse beside thee, so my soul doth ache.ן¿½

XXXII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus whispering, his warm, unnerved arm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sank in her pillow. Shaded was her dream
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the dusk curtains: ן¿½twas a midnight charm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Impossible to melt as iced stream:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lustrous salvers in the moonlight gleam;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Broad golden fringe upon the carpet lies:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It seemן¿½d he never, never could redeem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From such a stedfast spell his ladyן¿½s eyes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So musן¿½d awhile, entoilן¿½d in woofed phantasies.

XXXIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Awakening up, he took her hollow lute,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tumultuous, and, in chords that tenderest be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He playן¿½d an ancient ditty, long since mute,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Provence callן¿½d, ן¿½La belle dame sans mercy:ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Close to her ear touching the melody;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherewith disturbן¿½d, she utterן¿½d a soft moan:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He ceased, she panted quick and suddenly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her blue affrayed eyes wide open shone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon his knees he sank, pale as smooth-sculptured stone.

XXXIV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her eyes were open, but she still beheld,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now wide awake, the vision of her sleep:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a painful change, that nigh expellן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The blisses of her dream so pure and deep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At which fair Madeline began to weep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And moan forth witless words with many a sigh;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While still her gaze on Porphyro would keep;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who knelt, with joined hands and piteous eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fearing to move or speak, she lookן¿½d so dreamingly.

XXXV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ah, Porphyro!ן¿½ said she, ן¿½but even now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thy voice was at sweet tremble in mine ear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Made tuneable with every sweetest vow;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And those sad eyes were spiritual and clear:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How changן¿½d thou art! how pallid, chill, and drear!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Give me that voice again, my Porphyro,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Those looks immortal, those complainings dear!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh leave me not in this eternal woe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For if thou diest, my Love, I know not where to go.ן¿½

XXXVI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beyond a mortal man impassionן¿½d far
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At these voluptuous accents, he arose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ethereal, flushן¿½d, and like a throbbing star
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seen mid the sapphire heavenן¿½s deep repose;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into her dream he melted, as the rose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blendeth its odour with the violet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Solution sweet: meantime the frost-wind blows
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like Loveן¿½s alarum pattering the sharp sleet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the window-panes; St. Agnesן¿½ moon hath set.

XXXVII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis dark: quick pattereth the flaw-blown sleet:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This is no dream, my bride, my Madeline!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis dark: the iced gusts still rave and beat:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No dream, alas! alas! and woe is mine!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Porphyro will leave me here to fade and pine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Cruel! what traitor could thee hither bring?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I curse not, for my heart is lost in thine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Though thou forsakest a deceived thing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A dove forlorn and lost with sick unpruned wing.ן¿½

XXXVIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My Madeline! sweet dreamer! lovely bride!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Say, may I be for aye thy vassal blest?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thy beautyן¿½s shield, heart-shapן¿½d and vermeil dyed?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ah, silver shrine, here will I take my rest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½After so many hours of toil and quest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A famishן¿½d pilgrim, saved by miracle.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Though I have found, I will not rob thy nest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saving of thy sweet self; if thou thinkן¿½st well
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To trust, fair Madeline, to no rude infidel.ן¿½

XXXIX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hark! ן¿½Tis an elfin-storm from faery land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of haggard seeming, but a boon indeed:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Arise, arise! the morning is at hand;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The bloated wassaillers will never heed:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Let us away, my love, with happy speed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There are no ears to hear, or eyes to see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Drownן¿½d all in Rhenish and the sleepy mead:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Awake! arise! my love, and fearless be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For oן¿½er the southern moors I have a home for thee.ן¿½

XL

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She hurried at his words, beset with fears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For there were sleeping dragons all around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At glaring watch, perhaps, with ready spears
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down the wide stairs a darkling way they found.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In all the house was heard no human sound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A chain-droopן¿½d lamp was flickering by each door;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The arras, rich with horseman, hawk, and hound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flutterן¿½d in the besieging windן¿½s uproar;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the long carpets rose along the gusty floor.

XLI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They glide, like phantoms, into the wide hall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like phantoms, to the iron porch, they glide;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where lay the Porter, in uneasy sprawl,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a huge empty flaggon by his side;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wakeful bloodhound rose, and shook his hide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his sagacious eye an inmate owns:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By one, and one, the bolts full easy slide:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The chains lie silent on the footworn stones;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The key turns, and the door upon its hinges groan.

XLII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they are gone: ay, ages long ago
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These lovers fled away into the storm.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That night the Baron dreamt of many a woe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all his warrior-guests, with shade and form
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of witch, and demon, and large coffin-worm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were long be-nightmarן¿½d. Angela the old
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Died palsy-twitchן¿½d, with meagre face deform;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Beadsman, after thousand aves told,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For aye unsought for slept among his ashes cold.
meaning: About: John Keats (31 October 1795 ן¿½ 23 February 1821) was one of the principal poets of the English Romantic movement. During his short life, his work received constant critical attacks from the periodicals of the day, but his posthumous influence on poets such as Alfred Tennyson has been immense. Elaborate word choice and sensual imagery characterize Keats's poetry, including a series of odes that were his masterpieces and which remain among the most popular poems in English literature. Keats's letters, which expound on his aethestic theory of "negative capability", are among the most celebrated by any writer.
